learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of contemporary research about pedagogical approaches and student understandings in relation to mathematics. (APST 2.1)

Apply knowledge and understanding of mathematical concepts, substance and structures (number, algebra, measurement, and geometry) and demonstrate the supporting effective teaching strategies used to develop students’ depth of conceptual knowledge in mathematics. (APST 2.1; 3.3)

Apply knowledge and use of curriculum, assessment and reporting knowledge to design learning sequences and lesson plans demonstrating a range of effective teaching strategies responsive to learning strengths and needs of all students from diverse linguistic, cultural, religious and socio-economic backgrounds. (APST 1.3, 2.3, 3.2).

Identify, explain and use a range of appropriate resources (mathematical language, symbols, hands-on manipulatives), including ICT as tools to design effective learning sequences to support and engage students’ mathematical learning. (APST 2.1, 2.3, 2.5, 2.6, 3.4)

Critically apply theories and current research about primary students development of mathematical concepts and proficiencies to create organised learning experiences, including challenging learning goals, for all students of varying ability in mathematics as required by Australian Curriculum: Mathematics. (APST 2.3, 3.1)

Apply personal mathematical content and pedagogical knowledge to critically reflect on the appropriate curriculum documents and the effectiveness of different approaches to teaching mathematics that cater for students across a full range of abilities, particularly the formative, summative and diagnostic uses of assessment data, and apply these approaches to support students diverse learning needs. (APST 1.3, 2.3, 2.5, 5.1)
Demonstrate the capacity to analyse and interpret student assessment data to evaluate student learning and modify teaching practice. (APST 5.4)

assessments.weighting: <dd>30% 15 minutes total (12 minute presentation + 3 min questions) </dd>
assessments.due_date:
10/08/2026 - 31/08/2026

5/10/2026 - 26/10/2026

Teaching demonstrations will be conducted during tutorial class time from Weeks 3-6 and then from weeks 10-13.
assessments.task_description:
Rationale: Teachers in primary school settings are expected to effectively communicate mathematical ideas and respond to student thinking in real time. This assessment task provides an opportunity for you to plan and deliver a micro teaching demonstration that showcases your ability to translate mathematical concepts into clear, engaging, and purposeful instruction. Through this process, you will develop the practical skills needed to support diverse learners and enact responsive, high-quality mathematics teaching.

Task:

This is an individual assessment task which requires you to plan and deliver a teaching demonstration.

You will present one of the mathematics learning experience/activities you have designed as part of the portfolio (Assessment 3) to a small group. This provides you with the opportunity to practice teaching mathematics.

At the beginning of the Semester you will be allocated a week for your teaching demonstration. Your teaching demonstration is only on the topic that was taught the week prior. For example, if your teaching demonstration is in Week 3 you will demonstrate an early number activity (week 2 content taught). If your teaching demonstration is in week 11, you will focus on a measurement lesson for early primary contexts (week 10 content taught).

The teaching demonstration should take a maximum of 15 minutes and consist of:

  • a brief overview which outlines the topic you are teaching, linking to the curriculum, year level of students, and teaching strategies you are employing (approx. 1-2 minutes)
  • delivery of a mathematics learning experience/activity (approx. 10 minutes).
  • Answer questions from the teaching staff and peers related to your micro teaching demonstration (approx. 3 minutes)


If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in preparing the Teaching Demonstration (Assessment Task 1), you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate referencing and acknowledgement, including an example coversheet to provide details of use, are available here: Overview - AI tools for assignments - Library Guides at University of Queensland Library. However, the Q&A section of the Teaching Demonstration (Assessment Task 1) is designed to protect academic integrity and assess your understanding of course concepts. You will be expected to satisfactorily respond to pertinent questions about your teaching demonstration spontaneously, without the use of AI or other materials or tools. You must only use the approved materials and tools for the Q&A section of the task. Use of prohibited materials in this section may be investigated and treated as academic misconduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Teaching demonstrations will be conducted in person during tutorial class time from weeks 3-6 and then from weeks 10-13.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Using student data to design consecutive lessons
assessments.task_description:
Rationale: To best inform the teaching and learning of students, teachers need to use appropriate assessment tools/strategies to gain insights into the mathematical thinking of their students. In addition, effective teachers also need to seek to meet the learning needs of students by planning lessons that utilise assessment data. As such, teachers gather assessment data about student learning, interpret the data, respond to the data by planning purposeful learning experiences, and then reflect upon the effectiveness of the mathematics teaching and learning.

Task: This assessment task is an individual task that requires you to interview one primary school student (Foundation - Year 6) using a research-based interview protocol to assess where a student is at in their learning (mathematical understanding and thinking for a particular concept). You will administer the interview with the student, analyse and interpret the data, and write a report about the students’ understandings, drawing links between the knowledge they demonstrate and the mathematical strategies they use. In response to the data and the report, you will design an introductory lesson at an appropriate level with a second lesson to follow (two mathematics lessons to address a particular learning need for the student.) Finally, you will provide a rationale for your planning decisions, making references to appropriate research literature (CC 2.3.1 – practised).

Your task will include:

  • A brief introduction that states clearly the function and purpose of the paper (approx. 100 words)
  • The data collected in the assessment interview including student responses and your own annotations (image of the assessment record sheet)
  • A report statement reflecting the student’s mathematical understandings (approx. 250 words)
  • Two mathematics lesson plans that respond to the assessment data that you collected via the assessment interview (approx. 750 words each)
  • A rationale for the lesson plans including justifications for the choice of lesson content, citing specific responses from the student and the statements, and selected pedagogies, citing relevant academic readings (approx. 500 words)
  • A conclusion that summarises the main points made throughout the paper (approx. 200 words)
  • A reference list showing the texts that have been accessed to support the planning of the mathematics lessons.


If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in preparing the Assessment Task 2 (using student data to design consecutive lessons), you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate referencing and acknowledgement, including an example coversheet to provide details of use, are available here: Overview - AI tools for assignments - Library Guides at University of Queensland Library
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Portfolio of micro teaching for primary school mathematics
assessments.task_description:
Rationale: Teachers in primary school settings are increasingly required to show evidence of engagement in professional learning (AITSL, 2012). In many cases, portfolios can provide a way of proving that engagement in professional learning has taken place. This assessment task is an opportunity for you to produce a portfolio of mathematics teaching and learning experiences that highlight your professional learning as a pre-service teacher.

Task: This task is an individual assessment task which requires you to develop a portfolio of mathematical learning experiences throughout the semester. For one of the learning experiences you design you will demonstrate this as a micro-teaching session to a small group of peers. This assessment is an opportunity for you to demonstrate the reflections and connections that you will make between mathematical content knowledge (MCK) and its connected pedagogical connect knowledge (PCK) to reflect upon the implications for your professional development as a teacher of mathematics.

You will:

  • Plan four learning experiences, selecting different year levels (P-6) and mathematical content areas (early number, place value, addition and subtraction, and/or multiplication and division, measurement, early algebra, geometry) to focus to develop a professional portfolio. Each learning experience should demonstrate your developing MCK and PCK by including:
  • A clear description of the organisation and progression of the learning experience
  • Identification of the key mathematical concept/s being targeted
  • Prior, current and future curricular connections
  • Anticipated student prior knowledge
  • Anticipated student misconceptions and difficulties
  • Anticipated student responses and strategies
  • Specific instructional actions you will take to support student understanding
  • Specific representations you will use to support student understanding
  • What and how student progress will be assessed during the learning experience
  • Specific actions you will take in response to this assessment
  • In addition:
  • Explain how ICT tools could be used to support student understanding
  • Explain the use of specific teaching strategies responsive to the learning strengths and needs of students from diverse linguistic, cultural, religious and socioeconomic backgrounds.
  • Draw upon contemporary research, demonstrate your understanding of the connections between the concepts, substance and structure of the content (MCK) and pedagogical content knowledge (PCK) when teaching in this content area. In doing so reflect upon the implications for your professional development as a teacher of mathematics in this content area.

Note: You will choose each year level once across the portfolio showing your capacity to plan and develop learning experiences for lower, middle and upper primary school students.


If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in preparing the Portfolio of Micro teaching (Assessment Task 3), you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate referencing and acknowledgement, including an example coversheet to provide details of use, are available here: Overview - AI tools for assignments - Library Guides at University of Queensland Library

learning_activities.topic:
Mathematical and pedagogical knowledge

Lecture - Mathematical and pedagogical knowledge:

The lecture this week will focus on understanding how you will develop confidence and competence as a teacher of mathematics in your mathematics courses. In doing so, we will explore how your competence and confidence will improve by developing your understanding of 1. various teaching strategies (both general and content specific), 2. how children think about mathematics, 3. the mathematics curriculum and 4. mathematics concepts, skills, and strategies (CC 2.5.3 - taught). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L06, L07


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Mathematical and pedagogical knowledge Lecture - Mathematical and pedagogical knowledge: The lecture this week will focus on understanding how you will develop confidence and competence as a teacher of mathematics in your mathematics courses. In doing so, we will explore how your competence and confidence will improve by developing your understanding of 1. various teaching strategies (both general and content specific), 2. how children think about mathematics, 3. the mathematics curriculum and 4. mathematics concepts, skills, and strategies (CC 2.5.3 - taught).

learning_activities.topic:
Mathematical and pedagogical knowledge

Tutorial - Mathematical and pedagogical knowledge:

In this tutorial you will critically reflect upon your current perception of mathematics education, and further explore modern mathematics pedagogy. We will also explore your strengths and limitations related to specific content areas in mathematics to plot a pathway toward confidence and capability. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L07


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Early number and Place Value

Lecture - Early number and Place Value:

The lecture this week will focus on the concepts needed to teach early number to primary school students including the development of counting systems; subitising, place value knowledge; development of number sense. In addition to this, we will examine the Australian Mathematics Curriculum in relation to early number concepts (CC 2.5.3 - taught). (APST: 2,1; 3.3) Learning outcomes: L01, L02


Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.topic:
Assessment for learning in mathematics

Lecture - Assessment for learning in mathematics:

This lecture focuses on assessment in mathematics. In particular, different types of assessments in mathematics, assessment for learning; assessment of learning; the impact of assessment on students learning; the integral nature of planning and assessment; assessment scales; and NAPLAN. These tasks are discussed in terms of their capacity to reveal not only procedural fluency but also conceptual understanding, strategy use, and mathematical dispositions (CC 2.3.1 – taught). (APST 1.3; 2.3; 2.5; 3.2; 5.1; 5.4) Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L07, L08

Learning outcomes: L03, L06, L07

These tasks are discussed in terms of their capacity to reveal not only procedural fluency but also conceptual understanding, strategy use, and mathematical dispositions (CC 2.3.1 – taught). (APST 1.3; 2.3; 2.5; 3.2; 5.1; 5.4)

Assessment for learning in mathematics

learning_activities.topic:
Strategies for planning mathematics lessons

Lecture - Strategies for planning mathematics lessons:

This lecture will focus on the pedagogical approaches for mathematics. In addition to this we will focus on how to plan for sequential learning in the mathematics classroom and numeracy across the curriculum. Preservice teachers learn about the importance of providing opportunities for problem solving with familiar content independent of the teacher, progressively building in challenge, so that their students have multiple practice opportunities for metacognitively managing problem solving, and retrieving the propositional, procedural and contextual knowledge of problem-solving strategies (CC 2.5.4 – taught). (APST 1.3; 2.1; 2.3; 2.5; 2.6; 3.1; 3.2; 3.4; 5.4) Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Preservice teachers learn about the importance of providing opportunities for problem solving with familiar content independent of the teacher, progressively building in challenge, so that their students have multiple practice opportunities for metacognitively managing problem solving, and retrieving the propositional, procedural and contextual knowledge of problem-solving strategies (CC 2.5.4 – taught). This lecture will focus on the fundamental concepts with regards to early algebraic thinking for primary school students (patterning, equivalence, and functional thinking). In addition, we will consider patterning to algebra and generalised arithmetic; the impact of the equals sign and other symbols on algebraic conceptual knowledge development (CC 2.5.3 - taught). (APST 2.1; 3.3) Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.topic:
Transformations, Position and location

Lecture - Transformations, Position and location:

This week will focus on developing the skills for teaching transformational geometry, position and location in the primary school (CC 2.5.3 - taught). (APST 2.1, 2.3, 2.5, 2.6, 3.3, 3.4) Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Transformations, Position and location

Tutorial - Transformations, Position and location:

This tutorial will focus on the teaching and learning approaches for transformational geometry, position and location. We will be examining the curriculum and designing learning activities. (APST 2.1, 2.3, 2.5, 2.6, 3.1; 3.3; 3.4; 5.1) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06, L07
